design_philosophy.core_loop: Capture/invent robots → equip and customize them → engage in turn-based ATB battles → earn scrap to craft better equipment → progress through story
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management (scrap collection and item combination) balanced against combat effectiveness; player agency in robot customization vs. experimentation risk
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a young robotics inventor building and commanding personalized robot companions; exercise creative control over robot attributes, aesthetics, and combat tactics
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects traditional RPG stat progression—the player manually programs attributes and special attacks via 'macro-like' commands rather than relying on level-up curves; emphasizes invention/crafting as core gameplay rather than ancillary; non-combat character (protagonist) directs three robots instead of fighting directly, similar to monster-taming but predates Pokémon's commercial success

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player controls robot equipment, attribute distribution via Program Points, special attack macros, and robot switching strategy. Difficulty scales with player's investment in crafting and customization; poorly equipped robots increase challenge significantly

progression.upgrade_system: Dual-layer upgrade: (1) Equipment crafting via Invention Machine using Scrap (12 types: Scrap 1-10, A, B) and item combinations; (2) Robot customization through Program Points allocation to attributes and special attack programming. Weapons strengthen through combining identical types (up to 9 levels). Higher-level Inventor's Friend recipes unlock based on protagonist's level
progression.unlock_mechanism: Level-gated recipe access; certain Scrap types and item combinations locked until protagonist reaches thresholds. Story progression unlocks new areas and enemy encounters
replayability_hooks: Three robot slots allow different composition strategies on replay
Customizable robot aesthetics (colors, names) encourage personal attachment
Item combination permutations create multiple viable equipment paths
Time-limit bonuses during battles reward speedrun optimization

world_building_techniques: Environmental context (peaceful planet Quintenix disrupted by The Hackers faction)
Character relationships (protagonist as son of famous inventor Dr. Akihabara; ancestral lore with Rask and Gateau)
Temporal world-building (Tetron stone enabling time observation and travel; ancient conflict between Rask and Gateau seeding current events)
Faction opposition (The Hackers vs. society; Gateau's universal domination goal)

boss_design.philosophy: Story-integrated final bosses tied to factional conflict (The Hackers, Gateau). Limited detailed documentation of boss encounter structure available
notable_bosses: Gateau (final antagonist in space fortress; seeks Tetron for universal domination)

faction_indicators: The Hackers (primary antagonist faction seeking Dr. Akihabara and the Tetron)
danger_signals: The Hackers' sudden uprising breaks long peace
Tetron's time-travel power presented as universe-control risk
Gateau's plan for 'universal domination' escalates threat scope
environmental_storytelling: Planet Quintenix (Paradise Star) as peaceful baseline contrasted with Hacker disruption
Rococo town as initial disruption epicenter
Multiple geographic locations across planet (Choco/Chocolate Star as ancestral connection)
Space fortress as Gateau's power seat
